@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/self-care / grooming / sensory processing / occupational therapy/haircut tolerance / auditory-tactile sensitivity / grooming readiness / sensory preparation / anxiety management/9-materials-that-help-with-haircut-preparation#faq

mainEntity.name: Why does haircuts have become one of the most dreaded experiences in our family. it's not just that my child dislikes them — they are genuinely terrified. the fear starts days before when i mention we need to get a haircut. they beg me not to go. they hide. they cry. on the day itself, getting them into the car is a battle. getting them into the salon is worse. and once we're inside? complete meltdown. they won't sit in the chair. the cape around their neck makes them panic — they say it's choking them, even when it's loose. they can't tolerate anyone touching their head. when the clippers turn on, the buzzing sound sends them over the edge. they scream that it hurts, that the vibration is unbearable. hair falling on their face, their neck, their arms — each piece makes them flinch and cry harder. they squirm and jerk away, and i'm terrified they'll get cut by the scissors. i've tried everything i can think of. bringing their tablet. promising treats afterward. going to kid-friendly salons with cartoon chairs. nothing works once they're actually in the chair experiencing it. some stylists have been patient, but most get frustrated. i've been told not to come back. i've had other customers complain. i've left with my child sobbing and their hair half-cut, looking worse than when we arrived. now i avoid haircuts as long as possible. their hair gets so long that kids at school comment. teachers have mentioned it. i feel judged, like i can't handle a basic parenting task that other families do without thinking. i've tried cutting it at home, but without another adult to help hold them still, it's impossible — and dangerous. i'm not skilled with scissors near a moving target. the home attempts have resulted in disasters that needed professional fixing, which means salon trauma anyway. i don't understand why this is so hard for them. other kids sit through haircuts just fine. i know they're not being dramatic on purpose — i can see the real terror in their eyes. something about the sensory experience is genuinely overwhelming. they need a way to be prepared for this, to build tolerance before we're in the salon chair. they need the haircut experience to feel safe instead of terrifying. i need to not dread this basic grooming task anymore?

acceptedAnswer.text: HAIRCUT-RELATED SENSORY ANXIETY / MULTI-SENSORY DEFENSIVENESS TO GROOMING STIMULI / AUDITORY-TACTILE OVER-RESPONSIVITY / ANTICIPATORY ANXIETY RELATED TO GROOMING PROCEDURES! Haircuts involve simultaneous multi-sensory input that can be overwhelming for children with sensory processing differences: auditory input (clippers buzzing, scissors snipping, salon noise), tactile input (cape on neck, hands on head, hair falling on skin, vibration of clippers), vestibular input (sitting still in elevated chair), visual input (mirrors, movement around them), and olfactory input (salon products). For children with sensory over-responsivity — particularly to auditory and tactile stimuli — these combined inputs can trigger a fight-or-flight response that manifests as extreme distress, meltdowns, and avoidance. The unpredictability of the experience (not knowing when clippers will touch, when hair will fall, how long it will last) and loss of control amplify anxiety. Past negative experiences create anticipatory trauma that begins long before the actual haircut. Occupational therapy addresses haircut tolerance through systematic desensitization, sensory preparation, environmental modifications, and building gradual familiarity with each component of the haircut experience.

mainEntity.name: What is Haircut-Related Sensory Anxiety / Multi-Sensory Defensiveness to Grooming Stimuli / Auditory-Tactile Over-Responsivity / Anticipatory Anxiety Related to Grooming Procedures?

acceptedAnswer.text: Haircuts involve simultaneous multi-sensory input that can be overwhelming for children with sensory processing differences: auditory input (clippers buzzing, scissors snipping, salon noise), tactile input (cape on neck, hands on head, hair falling on skin, vibration of clippers), vestibular input (sitting still in elevated chair), visual input (mirrors, movement around them), and olfactory input (salon products). For children with sensory over-responsivity — particularly to auditory and tactile stimuli — these combined inputs can trigger a fight-or-flight response that manifests as extreme distress, meltdowns, and avoidance. The unpredictability of the experience (not knowing when clippers will touch, when hair will fall, how long it will last) and loss of control amplify anxiety. Past negative experiences create anticipatory trauma that begins long before the actual haircut. Occupational therapy addresses haircut tolerance through systematic desensitization, sensory preparation, environmental modifications, and building gradual familiarity with each component of the haircut experience. Common signs include: Extreme anxiety or meltdowns when haircut is mentioned, Crying, screaming, or attempting to flee during haircuts, Cannot tolerate cape around neck (reports choking sensation), Covers ears or becomes distressed when clippers turn on, Reports that clippers 'hurt' or vibration is unbearable.

mainEntity.name: How does a Haircut Social Story Book help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Social stories provide predictability by showing exactly what will happen during a haircut, in what order, and what the child can expect at each step. For children with autism or anxiety, unpredictability is a major source of distress — they don't know what's coming next, how long it will last, or what each sensation will feel like. A well-designed haircut social story walks through the entire experience: arriving at the salon, waiting, sitting in the chair, wearing the cape, spray bottle, scissors, clippers, hair falling, getting brushed off, and leaving. It normalizes the experience ('Many kids feel nervous about haircuts'), includes coping strategies ('When I hear the clippers, I can take deep breaths'), and shows a positive outcome. Reading the social story repeatedly before the haircut creates mental familiarity — the experience becomes 'known' rather than terrifying unknown. Price: ₹200–600. DIY option: Create a personalized social story using photos of your actual salon, your child's stylist (if consistent), and your child. Include: 1) We're going for a haircut, 2) This is the salon, 3) I'll sit in a special chair, 4) I'll wear a cape, 5) The stylist will use scissors/clippers, 6) Hair will fall — it doesn't hurt, 7) When I feel uncomfortable, I can [coping strategy], 8) When it's done, my hair looks nice, 9) Then we'll [positive ending]. Use simple, positive language. Read daily in the week before the haircut. Laminate pages or use a photo book format. Safety: Keep language positive and empowering, not frightening. Focus on coping and success, not on potential difficulties.

mainEntity.name: How does a Practice Cape (Salon or Homemade) help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Many children with sensory sensitivities cannot tolerate the salon cape — they report feeling choked, trapped, or restricted even when the cape is loose. This tactile defensiveness around the neck (a vulnerable area) triggers panic that can derail the entire haircut before scissors even appear. Practice wearing a cape at home, outside the stressful salon context, allows gradual desensitization. Start with brief exposure (just draping on shoulders), then gradually increase duration and neck closure. Pair cape-wearing with positive activities at home (watching a favorite video, playing a game). By the time the child enters the salon, the cape sensation is familiar rather than novel and threatening. Some children do better with alternative cover-ups (towel, their own large t-shirt) which salons may accommodate. Price: ₹100–400. DIY option: Purchase an inexpensive salon cape or make one from smooth fabric (avoid scratchy materials). Start with just draping cape over shoulders without fastening — pair with preferred activity (video, game, treat). Gradually increase time wearing cape. Progress to fastening loosely at neck, then typical tightness. Practice 2-3 times weekly in weeks before haircut. Some children tolerate certain fabrics better — experiment with silky vs. cotton. Alternative: practice with large towel tucked at neck if child will use towel at salon instead. Safety: Never force cape wearing. If child is highly distressed, go slower — cape presence nearby first, then touching shoulders, etc. Forcing creates negative associations.

mainEntity.name: How does a Toy or Practice Clippers help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: The buzzing sound and vibration of clippers are often the most distressing elements of haircuts for sensory-sensitive children. The sound is loud, unpredictable, and right next to their ears. The vibration against the scalp can feel painful or overwhelming for children with tactile over-responsivity. Practice clippers — which may be toy versions that make sound, or real clippers with guard on used only for sound and vibration exposure — allow controlled desensitization at home. The child can hold the clippers, control when they turn on, feel the vibration on their hands before their head, and become familiar with the sound. What's familiar is less threatening. Some families progress from clippers near child, to touching arm, to touching back of neck, to touching hairline — never forcing, always child-paced. Price: ₹150–600. DIY option: Option 1: Purchase toy grooming set with pretend clippers that make sound. Option 2: Use real hair clippers with guard attachment. Start with clippers off — let child hold, explore. Then turn on across the room — child controls off switch. Gradually move closer. Progress to vibration on child's hand, then arm, then shoulder, then neck, then hairline — only as tolerated, never forced. Pair with positive rewards. Electric toothbrush provides similar vibration practice if clippers too threatening initially. Safety: If using real clippers for practice, use guard attachment and never pretend to cut — practice is for sensory exposure only. Actual cutting at home requires separate careful approach.

mainEntity.name: How does a Noise-Reducing Headphones or Ear Defenders help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: For children with auditory over-responsivity, the sounds of the salon — clippers buzzing, scissors snipping, hair dryers, background noise, conversation — can be overwhelming and painful. Noise-reducing headphones or ear defenders decrease the volume of these sounds to a tolerable level without eliminating them entirely. This allows the child to experience the haircut with reduced auditory assault. Many children who cannot tolerate haircuts without headphones can sit calmly with them. Over time, as tolerance builds, some children can reduce reliance on headphones, but many continue using them indefinitely as an accommodation — which is completely acceptable. Headphones that also play music or audio provide additional distraction and a sense of control over what they're hearing. Price: ₹500–2,500. DIY option: Purchase noise-reducing headphones (over-ear style provides best coverage) or ear defenders designed for sensory-sensitive children. Test at home first — some children dislike the pressure on ears, so find comfortable fit. Practice wearing during other activities so headphones are familiar. For haircuts: put headphones on before entering salon, keep on throughout. Some children prefer headphones with audio (music, audiobook) for additional distraction and control. Communicate with stylist that headphones will stay on throughout. Safety: Ensure headphones don't interfere with stylist's ability to cut safely around ears. Some styles work better than others. Communicate with stylist.

mainEntity.name: How does a Visual Timer for Predictability help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not knowing how long an uncomfortable experience will last dramatically increases distress. When a child doesn't know if the haircut will take 5 minutes or 50 minutes, anxiety spirals. Visual timers provide concrete, visible evidence that the experience has a defined endpoint. The child can see time passing and know exactly when it will be over. This predictability is profoundly calming for anxious children. For haircuts, the timer can show total expected time, or can be used in segments ('We'll do 5 minutes of cutting, then a break'). Short segments with breaks between may be more tolerable than one long session. Visual timers work better than verbal promises because the child can verify the information themselves rather than relying on adult promises that may have been broken before. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Use a Time Timer, sand timer, or phone app with visual countdown. For haircuts: estimate realistic duration with stylist, set timer where child can see it. Announce: 'The timer shows how much longer. When it reaches zero, we're done.' For very anxious children: use shorter segments with breaks. Example: 5-minute timer, then break, then another 5 minutes. Honor the timer absolutely — if haircut isn't finished when timer ends, stop anyway and schedule continuation. Building trust in the timer is essential. Safety: Always honor the timer. If you continue past timer end, trust is broken and timer becomes meaningless for future use. Start with conservative time estimates.

mainEntity.name: How does a Weighted Lap Pad or Compression Vest help with haircut-related sensory anxiety?

acceptedAnswer.text: Deep pressure input is calming and organizing for the nervous system, particularly for children with sensory processing differences. Weighted lap pads provide proprioceptive input that helps regulate the nervous system during stressful experiences. A weighted lap pad during a haircut gives the child grounding, organizing input that counteracts the dysregulating effects of the haircut sensory experience. Some children prefer compression vests, which provide constant firm pressure around the torso. These tools work by activating the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing the fight-or-flight response. The weight or pressure also provides a focal point of 'good' sensory input that the child can attend to instead of the uncomfortable haircut sensations. Price: ₹800–2,500. DIY option: Purchase a weighted lap pad (typically 1-2 kg for children — approximately 10% of body weight for lap pads). Introduce at home first during other activities so it's familiar and associated with calm. Bring to salon and place on lap before haircut begins. Alternative: compression vest worn under clothes. Some children prefer a heavy stuffed animal on lap. The key is consistent, familiar deep pressure input during the stressful experience. Can combine with other calming strategies. Safety: Use appropriate weight — approximately 10% of body weight for lap pad. Do not use weighted items on neck or chest. Remove if child seems uncomfortable or restricted.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/self-care / grooming / sensory processing / occupational therapy/haircut tolerance / auditory-tactile sensitivity / grooming readiness / sensory preparation / anxiety management/9-materials-that-help-with-haircut-preparation#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Haircut Related Sensory Anxiety
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Haircut anxiety in sensory-sensitive children is a multi-sensory processing issue, not a behavioral issue. The haircut experience combines multiple challenging inputs simultaneously: auditory (clippers, scissors, salon noise), tactile (cape on neck, hands on head, hair falling on skin, clipper vibration), vestibular (sitting elevated, head positioned by another person), and visual (mirrors, unfamiliar environment, movement around them). For children with sensory over-responsivity, these combined inputs trigger a fight-or-flight response that manifests as extreme distress. The unpredictability of the experience — not knowing when sounds will occur, when touch will happen, or how long it will last — amplifies anxiety exponentially. Past negative experiences create anticipatory trauma. The intervention approach is systematic desensitization: breaking down the haircut experience into components, practicing each component at home in controlled conditions, making the unfamiliar familiar, and providing sensory supports during the actual haircut. Forcing through distress creates trauma and worsens future experiences. Gradual, prepared exposure builds genuine tolerance.

Occupational therapy addresses haircut tolerance through systematic desensitization, sensory preparation, environmental modifications, and building gradual familiarity with each component of the haircut experience. @graph.name: 9 Materials for Haircut Related Sensory Anxiety

item.name: Haircut Social Story Book
item.description: Social stories provide predictability by showing exactly what will happen during a haircut, in what order, and what the child can expect at each step. For children with autism or anxiety, unpredictability is a major source of distress — they don't know what's coming next, how long it will last, or what each sensation will feel like. A well-designed haircut social story walks through the entire experience: arriving at the salon, waiting, sitting in the chair, wearing the cape, spray bottle, scissors, clippers, hair falling, getting brushed off, and leaving. It normalizes the experience ('Many kids feel nervous about haircuts'), includes coping strategies ('When I hear the clippers, I can take deep breaths'), and shows a positive outcome. Reading the social story repeatedly before the haircut creates mental familiarity — the experience becomes 'known' rather than terrifying unknown.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a personalized social story using photos of your actual salon, your child's stylist (if consistent), and your child. Include: 1) We're going for a haircut, 2) This is the salon, 3) I'll sit in a special chair, 4) I'll wear a cape, 5) The stylist will use scissors/clippers, 6) Hair will fall — it doesn't hurt, 7) When I feel uncomfortable, I can [coping strategy], 8) When it's done, my hair looks nice, 9) Then we'll [positive ending]. Use simple, positive language. Read daily in the week before the haircut. Laminate pages or use a photo book format.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Keep language positive and empowering, not frightening. Focus on coping and success, not on potential difficulties.

item.name: Practice Cape (Salon or Homemade)
item.description: Many children with sensory sensitivities cannot tolerate the salon cape — they report feeling choked, trapped, or restricted even when the cape is loose. This tactile defensiveness around the neck (a vulnerable area) triggers panic that can derail the entire haircut before scissors even appear. Practice wearing a cape at home, outside the stressful salon context, allows gradual desensitization. Start with brief exposure (just draping on shoulders), then gradually increase duration and neck closure. Pair cape-wearing with positive activities at home (watching a favorite video, playing a game). By the time the child enters the salon, the cape sensation is familiar rather than novel and threatening. Some children do better with alternative cover-ups (towel, their own large t-shirt) which salons may accommodate.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Purchase an inexpensive salon cape or make one from smooth fabric (avoid scratchy materials). Start with just draping cape over shoulders without fastening — pair with preferred activity (video, game, treat). Gradually increase time wearing cape. Progress to fastening loosely at neck, then typical tightness. Practice 2-3 times weekly in weeks before haircut. Some children tolerate certain fabrics better — experiment with silky vs. cotton. Alternative: practice with large towel tucked at neck if child will use towel at salon instead.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never force cape wearing. If child is highly distressed, go slower — cape presence nearby first, then touching shoulders, etc. Forcing creates negative associations.

item.name: Toy or Practice Clippers
item.description: The buzzing sound and vibration of clippers are often the most distressing elements of haircuts for sensory-sensitive children. The sound is loud, unpredictable, and right next to their ears. The vibration against the scalp can feel painful or overwhelming for children with tactile over-responsivity. Practice clippers — which may be toy versions that make sound, or real clippers with guard on used only for sound and vibration exposure — allow controlled desensitization at home. The child can hold the clippers, control when they turn on, feel the vibration on their hands before their head, and become familiar with the sound. What's familiar is less threatening. Some families progress from clippers near child, to touching arm, to touching back of neck, to touching hairline — never forcing, always child-paced.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Option 1: Purchase toy grooming set with pretend clippers that make sound. Option 2: Use real hair clippers with guard attachment. Start with clippers off — let child hold, explore. Then turn on across the room — child controls off switch. Gradually move closer. Progress to vibration on child's hand, then arm, then shoulder, then neck, then hairline — only as tolerated, never forced. Pair with positive rewards. Electric toothbrush provides similar vibration practice if clippers too threatening initially.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: If using real clippers for practice, use guard attachment and never pretend to cut — practice is for sensory exposure only. Actual cutting at home requires separate careful approach.

item.name: Noise-Reducing Headphones or Ear Defenders
item.description: For children with auditory over-responsivity, the sounds of the salon — clippers buzzing, scissors snipping, hair dryers, background noise, conversation — can be overwhelming and painful. Noise-reducing headphones or ear defenders decrease the volume of these sounds to a tolerable level without eliminating them entirely. This allows the child to experience the haircut with reduced auditory assault. Many children who cannot tolerate haircuts without headphones can sit calmly with them. Over time, as tolerance builds, some children can reduce reliance on headphones, but many continue using them indefinitely as an accommodation — which is completely acceptable. Headphones that also play music or audio provide additional distraction and a sense of control over what they're hearing.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Purchase noise-reducing headphones (over-ear style provides best coverage) or ear defenders designed for sensory-sensitive children. Test at home first — some children dislike the pressure on ears, so find comfortable fit. Practice wearing during other activities so headphones are familiar. For haircuts: put headphones on before entering salon, keep on throughout. Some children prefer headphones with audio (music, audiobook) for additional distraction and control. Communicate with stylist that headphones will stay on throughout.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure headphones don't interfere with stylist's ability to cut safely around ears. Some styles work better than others. Communicate with stylist.

item.name: Visual Timer for Predictability
item.description: Not knowing how long an uncomfortable experience will last dramatically increases distress. When a child doesn't know if the haircut will take 5 minutes or 50 minutes, anxiety spirals. Visual timers provide concrete, visible evidence that the experience has a defined endpoint. The child can see time passing and know exactly when it will be over. This predictability is profoundly calming for anxious children. For haircuts, the timer can show total expected time, or can be used in segments ('We'll do 5 minutes of cutting, then a break'). Short segments with breaks between may be more tolerable than one long session. Visual timers work better than verbal promises because the child can verify the information themselves rather than relying on adult promises that may have been broken before.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use a Time Timer, sand timer, or phone app with visual countdown. For haircuts: estimate realistic duration with stylist, set timer where child can see it. Announce: 'The timer shows how much longer. When it reaches zero, we're done.' For very anxious children: use shorter segments with breaks. Example: 5-minute timer, then break, then another 5 minutes. Honor the timer absolutely — if haircut isn't finished when timer ends, stop anyway and schedule continuation. Building trust in the timer is essential.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Always honor the timer. If you continue past timer end, trust is broken and timer becomes meaningless for future use. Start with conservative time estimates.

item.name: Weighted Lap Pad or Compression Vest
item.description: Deep pressure input is calming and organizing for the nervous system, particularly for children with sensory processing differences. Weighted lap pads provide proprioceptive input that helps regulate the nervous system during stressful experiences. A weighted lap pad during a haircut gives the child grounding, organizing input that counteracts the dysregulating effects of the haircut sensory experience. Some children prefer compression vests, which provide constant firm pressure around the torso. These tools work by activating the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing the fight-or-flight response. The weight or pressure also provides a focal point of 'good' sensory input that the child can attend to instead of the uncomfortable haircut sensations.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Purchase a weighted lap pad (typically 1-2 kg for children — approximately 10% of body weight for lap pads). Introduce at home first during other activities so it's familiar and associated with calm. Bring to salon and place on lap before haircut begins. Alternative: compression vest worn under clothes. Some children prefer a heavy stuffed animal on lap. The key is consistent, familiar deep pressure input during the stressful experience. Can combine with other calming strategies.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use appropriate weight — approximately 10% of body weight for lap pad. Do not use weighted items on neck or chest. Remove if child seems uncomfortable or restricted.

item.name: Handheld Fidget or Stress Ball
item.description: Giving the hands something to do provides an outlet for nervous energy and a focus point for attention during uncomfortable experiences. Fidgets and stress balls provide tactile input that can be calming and organizing. Squeezing a stress ball is incompatible with escalating panic — it provides a physical action that channels anxiety. Fidgets also give the child something they control during an experience where they have little control. The act of manipulating a fidget can occupy working memory, leaving less cognitive space for anxious thoughts. For haircuts, a fidget in the hands keeps hands busy (rather than grabbing at scissors or stylist), provides sensory input, and gives the child something to focus on other than the haircut sensations.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Provide a preferred fidget toy, stress ball, or sensory item that the child finds calming. Let them choose which to bring. Introduce and practice using fidgets at home during other mildly stressful situations so the strategy is familiar. During haircut: place fidget in hands before cape goes on, encourage use throughout. Some children prefer specific textures — squishy, bumpy, smooth. Have backup fidgets in case one drops. Simple stress ball or theraputty work well.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure fidget is quiet (not distracting to stylist) and doesn't require visual attention (should be something child can use while looking straight ahead for haircut).

item.name: Preferred Video or Tablet with Headphones
item.description: Distraction is a legitimate and effective coping strategy, particularly when paired with other sensory accommodations. A tablet or phone playing a highly preferred video captures visual and auditory attention, leaving less cognitive space for processing the haircut sensations. For some children, a preferred video is sufficiently absorbing that they barely notice the haircut happening. This strategy works best when: the content is highly preferred and absorbing, headphones provide auditory focus and mask salon sounds, and other sensory modifications (cape desensitization, weighted lap pad) address the worst triggers. The tablet also gives the child something they control in an otherwise uncontrollable situation. Over time, as tolerance builds, distraction can potentially be reduced — but many families continue using it indefinitely as a successful accommodation.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Use existing tablet or phone with highly preferred content — something the child loves and finds absorbing. Pair with over-ear headphones (ideally noise-reducing). Position device at child's eye level using a stand or have parent hold it. Start video before haircut begins. Volume loud enough to mask salon sounds but not harmful to hearing. Have backup videos in case one ends. Ensure device is charged. Some salons have mounted tablets — but child's own device with preferred content usually works better.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Ensure headphone volume is safe (not damaging to hearing). Balance absorption with awareness — child should still be able to follow basic instructions from stylist if needed.

item.name: Neck Brush and Spray Bottle for Home Practice
item.description: Two common haircut triggers are the spray bottle (unexpected cold water hitting face, head, or dripping down neck) and the neck brush (soft bristles brushing away cut hair, which can be ticklish or irritating). Practicing with these tools at home allows desensitization in a controlled, child-paced environment. The child can hold the spray bottle and control when water sprays, experiencing the sensation on their own terms. They can feel the neck brush on their arms, shoulders, and neck before encountering it at the salon. Home practice transforms these from unknown, unpredictable sensations into familiar, tolerable ones. The child learns what to expect and develops tolerance before the actual haircut adds pressure.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Purchase a fine-mist spray bottle and soft barber's neck brush (or similar soft brush). Start with play: let child spray water (on themselves, on a mirror, anywhere). Make it fun and controlled by them. Gradually introduce spraying hair, face if they're ready. For neck brush: practice on arms, shoulders first. Progress to neck and hairline. Make it a game or pair with preferred activity. Practice multiple times in weeks before haircut. By haircut day, these sensations should be familiar and tolerable.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Never force practice if child is distressed. Keep it playful. If child has extreme aversion to water on face, work on that separately with OT guidance.

socialMedia.caption: When the salon chair feels like a torture seat. For sensory-sensitive kids, haircuts are genuinely overwhelming — sounds, sensations, unpredictability. Here are 9 materials that help prepare them: social stories, practice cape, toy clippers, noise-reducing headphones, visual timer, weighted lap pad, fidgets, tablet with headphones, and spray bottle practice. Preparation before the salon changes everything. 🔖 #HaircutPreparation #SensoryProcessing #PinnacleBlooms
